title: Water trough maintenance
subtitle: How to keep livestock water troughs clean, functioning, and frost-protected.
excerpt: A dirty or frozen water trough is one of the most common causes of voluntary water restriction in livestock, which leads to reduced feed intake, production loss, and in sheep, urinary calculi. Fifteen minutes per trough per week prevents most problems.
sourceNotes: AHDB, livestock water quality guidance. NADIS, water provision factsheet. Scottish Government, livestock water requirements technical note.

content.text: Livestock water troughs need a weekly inspection and a monthly or bi-monthly scrub during warm months when algae and biofilm build up quickly. The
is the most common failure point and should be checked at every visit.

Confirm the trough is full or filling. Push the float down briefly; the valve should open and water should flow. Release; the flow should stop as the float rises. If the valve does not open or does not shut off, disassemble and inspect: the float may have taken on water and lost buoyancy, or the valve seat may be fouled with scale or debris. Feel the underside of the trough lid and inside the box for mould or slime.

Close the supply or hold the valve open to empty the trough. Scrub the interior with a stiff brush and clean water. Do not use household cleaning products; residues are unpalatable to livestock. A dilute bleach solution (1 part bleach to 10 parts water) can be used for heavily fouled troughs, provided the trough is rinsed thoroughly and flushed before refilling. Rinse until no bleach smell remains.

In sustained frost, water supply pipes and the trough contents will freeze. Lag exposed supply pipes with foam lagging sleeves before the first frost of the year. Insulated trough covers reduce heat loss overnight. In severe cold, break ice from the surface at each check and check the supply pipe has not frozen. A trough with a slow trickle of running water through it is less likely to freeze than a static one.

Site troughs on well-drained ground or on a concrete apron. Wet, poached ground around a trough is a welfare and foot health risk for all stock, particularly sheep prone to foot rot. Ensure all animals in the group can access the trough simultaneously without dominant animals monopolising it; a single trough for a large group creates competition.
